text:
PARIS 芒聙聰 Sure, he芒聙聶s biased. But, as Michael Johnson芒聙聰200-meter artist and the event芒聙聶s world record holder for 13 years芒聙聰notes, that doesn芒聙聶t make him wrong. The 200, he argues, forms an ideal blend of sprint poetry, combining power, speed and endurance.脗

It芒聙聶s tactical and strategic, but only briefly, at one or two points in any race. It requires 芒聙聹different energy systems than the 100,芒聙聺 he says, which requires more endurance work in training and speed that芒聙聶s stillblurfast. The 200 is precision. It involves form, technique, horsepower and split-second decisions made at top speed. The strategy can change depending on which lane a competitor is in. The 200-meter race is part 100, part 400; brains and muscle equally important.脗

Before he broke the world record in the 200, Johnson primarily trained for the 400-meter run. More typically, 100-meter specialists extend their power into twice the distance. Many still struggle to adjust. They typically need to add endurance, along with understanding that they cannot sprint as fast they can in a straight line for twice the distance. They must decide, Johnson says, where 芒聙聹they want to put most of their speed, most of their energy.芒聙聺

芒聙聹But some people can run it all out, like I could, because of my 400-meter background and base,芒聙聺 Johnson says. 芒聙聹But also because I had extraordinary speed.芒聙聺 He says that last part dispassionately, because it芒聙聶s true, and it doesn芒聙聶t sound like bragging.

There芒聙聶s an art to this race. Most typically it centers on decisions, many made in the precious seconds spent navigating the turn. In most world-class competitions, the best 200 artists will make one technical decision, maybe two. Some are already better curve runners, and they will deploy more energy as the race turns, attempting to remain near or ahead of the pack. Others should be considered straightaway specialists, and they will blaze from the starting blocks, conserve as much energy as possible through the turn and then hit max velocity toward the finish line. By the time they get there, Johnson says, the race is over.

Every second matters, as does every strategic decision, every technical movement, every step on the purple track at Stade de France. Those 200-meter savants must understand themselves, as athletes, what they芒聙聶re capable of and what their competitors can do. The 200, he says, is trial and error芒聙聰and 芒聙聹error, at this level, means losses.芒聙聺

In the coming days in Paris, the art will take center stage in both the men芒聙聶s and women芒聙聶s 200-meter competitions. Noah Lyles芒聙聰owner of the fourth-best 200 time ever, 0.01 ahead of Johnson芒聙聶s former world record芒聙聰will take aim at the current mark, set by Jamaica芒聙聶s Usain Bolt in 2009. He芒聙聶ll need to shave 0.13 seconds off his best time, 19.31 seconds. He芒聙聶ll compete against a field of elite sprinters芒聙聰and against that clock.

In the women芒聙聶s final, American Gabby Thomas is now the gold-medal favorite, after two-time reigning 200-meter world championShericka Jackson of Jamaica pulled outof the semifinal heats right before the race. Thomas had never defeated Jackson in the 200.脗

All understand the art that Johnson is describing. Lyles, in fact, released a video on YouTube in June 2023. It runs for more than 10 minutes. It芒聙聶s equally informative and hilarious. It芒聙聶s technical, the art of the 200 broken into individual components.

This, because he is Lyles, is how it starts:So,first things first,the 200?It芒聙聶s a beautiful woman.You gotta treat it like such.It芒聙聶s not the 100.It芒聙聶s not hard.It芒聙聶s not aggressive.It芒聙聶s finesse.It芒聙聶s pretty.It芒聙聶s sexy.

Each component, added up, begins to prove his points. Lyles doesn芒聙聶t care which way any sprinter points their starting blocks. He prefers straight ahead; not in, toward the turn, or out, toward the stands. But that芒聙聶s a choice where only personal preference matters. He wants his body to shoot forward, straight ahead, for the first few steps. After those, he gauges where he芒聙聶s sprinting in his lane芒聙聰in the middle, always, is the ideal.

Past 10 meters, he芒聙聶s driving, legs churning, slowly 芒聙聹putting more force into the ground.芒聙聺

Around 20 meters, his body, hips and chest slowly begin to rise. No jerking, hence the ramp up.

After 30 meters, his hips come underneath him, his chest rises and his shoulders remain forward, as he drives down the track. At that point, he wants to be standing up, raising his knees high and stamping them right back down on the track like pistons. 芒聙聹Pushing your momentum down the track, almost as if, like, you芒聙聶re falling,芒聙聺 he says in the video.

He gauges, again, his placement within his lane. If he芒聙聶s too inside, he芒聙聶s 芒聙聹cutting that stride芒聙聺 too much, losing energy and speed. If he芒聙聶s outside in his lane, he芒聙聶s running 芒聙聹too much track.芒聙聺 Think: golfer who bombs drives but elongates holes by spraying them far right or far left.

At 50 meters, he must make 芒聙聹probably the most difficult decision芒聙聺 in that race.How am I going to stay in the middle of the lane? Many coaches, he says, tell sprinters to lean in at the turn. 芒聙聹That芒聙聶s not accurate.芒聙聺 He wants to turn his hips, gradually, and the comparison he invokes芒聙聰a car芒聙聶s headlights芒聙聰makes his point easy to understand, even though he calls them 芒聙聹light beams.芒聙聺 He holds both hands near his hips to demonstrate. Lean and those light beams point right, toward the stands. Gradually turn the hips and they point the same way as the body.

At 75 meters, maybe 80, 芒聙聹It芒聙聶s time to start putting power into the ground, as you芒聙聶re coming out of this turn,芒聙聺 he says. 芒聙聹If you let up, in this part of the turn, you will not get the effect of all the work you did back there.芒聙聺

Another check of lane positioning. Then, more power, more speed. 芒聙聹So we can continue to have that energy that we built up, flowing with us, going down the track. Now, this is where you芒聙聶re going to feel the best momentum.芒聙聺

As the turn gives way to the straightway, the final sprint toward the finish line, Lyles says many athletes 芒聙聹release芒聙聺 their body, meaning they start to relax. This can push their momentum outside of their lane, or force them to 芒聙聹cut芒聙聺 the turn at too drastic of an angle, slowing the very momentum they just created. 芒聙聹And, because you made that move down [on the transition from turn to straightaway], the track is gonna spit you out, almost like a slingshot. Think of NASCAR. They go up high on the hill, and they come back down. It芒聙聶s the exact feeling you芒聙聶re gonna feel.芒聙聺

Interestingly, Lyles breaks the 200 into two, separate 100-meter aims. At 30 meters of the second 100, he says, it芒聙聶s time to commit芒聙聰to body position, to knees hammering into the track, to keeping those shoulders forward. If those go back, energy escapes upward, unnecessarily. 芒聙聹I芒聙聶m gonna ask you: what芒聙聶s your favorite cloud? And, if you tell me, you failed.芒聙聺

At 180 meters, mental focus becomes paramount. Sprinters must follow what their coaches taught them, as close as possible to their chosen ideal form. In all likelihood, their top-end speed is gone. They have two choices: hold it, or slowly begin to slow. Even then, 芒聙聹It needs to look pretty, he says. 芒聙聹Again, the 200 needs to be finessed.芒聙聺

Finally, the dip. Not too early. Not too late. 芒聙聹The very two last meters,芒聙聺 Lyles says.

That芒聙聶s the art, his version, one that Al Joyner芒聙聰the longtime, respected track coach芒聙聰understands well. His late wife, Florence Griffith-Joyner, still holds the women芒聙聶s 200-meter world record (21.34 seconds), set at the Seoul Olympics in 1988. The women芒聙聶s race, he says, 芒聙聹will be amazing, ridiculous, because they芒聙聶resoready.芒聙聺

Thomas, her mother Jennifer Randall says, 芒聙聹was born athletic.芒聙聺 She took up soccer, karate, softball, lacrosse, golf and tennis. Even as a toddler, when Thomas scampered down a hallway, mom says it appeared as if 芒聙聹she was floating on air.芒聙聺

芒聙聹Superfast,芒聙聺 Randall says, 芒聙聹even then.芒聙聺

Thomas does a million things, impacts thousands of people and runs really fast while balancingan unfathomable docket of ambition. She went to Harvard, won bronze in the 200 at the Tokyo Games (and gold in the 4X100-meter relay), got her Master芒聙聶s degree from Texas, took silver at the most recent world championships, then dominated the U.S. Olympic trials back in June. She芒聙聶s famous, smart, fast as hell and nice. But that combination can mask what she芒聙聶s like on the track, especially in the 200. Summarized, in a word:fierce.

Her best friend, Ngozi Musa, who also ran the 200 and other sprints at Harvard alongside Thomas, says she always saw the smiling speedster precisely that way. In Thomas芒聙聶s freshman season, she finished sixth at Olympic trials but came across as polite and lovely afterward. But don芒聙聶t mistake that bearing for how shefelt. She was pissed.

芒聙聹She芒聙聶s a force to be reckoned with,芒聙聺 Musa says. 芒聙聹Sheattacksthe 200.芒聙聺

Johnson, another 200-meter artist, couldn芒聙聶t agree more. He calls Thomas a 芒聙聹peerless competitor, but one of those people who doesn芒聙聶t always wear that outside the track and can actually be a normal person.芒聙聺

芒聙聹You know, her and Noah are in interesting positions,芒聙聺 he says. 芒聙聹Sha芒聙聶Carri [Richardson], too. With each, there is tremendous talent, there is some success, but they haven芒聙聶t won the big one, so to speak, yet. That will be on their minds. I was there in 1996, because in 芒聙聶92, I was a world champion and heavy favorite to win the 200 at the Olympics, and I didn芒聙聶t win. You don芒聙聶t want that to be a burden.芒聙聺

Add that pressure to the art of the 200-meter race. For Lyles, Thomas or anyone else, the hope is that the art will yield one thing: gold.

Greg Bishop is a senior writer for Sports Illustrated who has covered every kind of sport and every major event across six continents for more than two decades. He previously worked for The Seattle Times and The New York Times. He is the co-author of two books: Jim Gray芒聙聶s memoir, 芒聙聹Talking to GOATs芒聙聺; and Laurent Duvernay Tardif芒聙聶s 芒聙聹Red Zone芒聙聺. Bishop has written for Showtime Sports, Prime Video and DAZN, and has been nominated for eight sports Emmys, winning two, both for production. He has completed more than a dozen documentary film projects, with a wide range of duties. Bishop, who graduated from the Newhouse School at Syracuse University, is based in Seattle.

title: The Art of Running the 200-Meter Race: Noah Lyles Breaks Down What It Takes to Win
description: The Olympic gold medalist shares his technique for the sprint that requires a special blend of power, speed, endurance and strategic decision-making.脗聽
